howItWorks.intro: DPI (dots per inch) and PPI (pixels per inch) measure how many dots or pixels fit in one linear inch of a printed image. Higher DPI means sharper prints.
rules: DPI is calculated as image pixels ÷ print size in inches for each dimension
300 DPI is the standard for high-quality photo prints and professional publishing
150 DPI is acceptable for viewing at arm's length (posters, large prints)
72 DPI is screen resolution only — not suitable for print
howItWorks.outro: For best results, always shoot at the highest resolution your camera supports and crop minimally. Upscaling a low-resolution image does not improve actual print quality.

faq.question: What is the difference between DPI and PPI?
faq.answer: PPI (pixels per inch) refers to the digital image resolution, while DPI (dots per inch) refers to the printer's output resolution. In practice, the terms are used interchangeably when discussing print quality from digital images.

faq.question: What DPI do I need for a good print?
faq.answer: 300 DPI is the gold standard for photo prints and publications. 150 DPI works for large prints viewed from a distance. Below 100 DPI, individual pixels become visible and the print looks pixelated.

faq.question: Can I increase the DPI of an image?
faq.answer: You can resample an image to a higher DPI in photo editing software, but this interpolates (guesses) new pixel data. It won't add real detail. The best approach is to start with a higher-resolution source image.

faq.question: What DPI should I use for a billboard?
faq.answer: Billboards are viewed from far away, so 15-45 DPI is typically sufficient. Large format prints like banners need 75-150 DPI depending on viewing distance.

faq.question: Does my phone camera have enough resolution for prints?
faq.answer: Most modern phones (12+ MP) can produce excellent 8×10 prints at 300+ DPI. A 12 MP image (4000×3000) yields 400 DPI at 10×7.5 inches.
